_meta.methodology: Extracted from JSONL session chunks and human-messages.json. Keyword counts are line-level matches (may include context summaries and system injections, not only pure human utterances). Session counts and human_message_count values are exact.

sessions_per_era.description: Number of coding sessions per development era, derived from JSONL chunk files

era1-seed.note: Empty array. Seed era pre-dates Claude Code session logging.

era2-explosion.note: Empty array. Explosion era pre-dates structured session capture.

peak_session.description: Deep architecture planning session - the consolidation sprint
Conversational UX deep-dive and model evaluation

era5-conversational.insight: Highest messages-per-session ratio. The conversational breakthrough era produced the densest human-agent dialogue.

era6-quiet.insight: Fewest sessions but highest per-session intensity. The quiet period was actually deep, sustained work.

era7-multimedia.insight: Voice + aesthetic features. Sessions were more directive/implementation-focused, less exploratory dialogue.

peak_session.description: The Crucible - dogfooding, landing page testing, brutally honest evaluation

era8-dogfood.insight: Peak frustration era. Session intensity was very high, concentrated on quality assurance and fixing core loop issues.

era9-bible.insight: Documentation and narrative mining. Calm, reflective sessions. The project settles into storytelling mode.

derived_insights: Development happened across 13 calendar days (Mar 20 - Apr 1, 2026), with gaps on Mar 25-28
Era 4 (Quality) had the most sessions (27) but era 5 (Conversational) had more total messages (329 vs 277)
Era 6 (Quiet) had the fewest sessions (2) but the highest per-session message count (74.0 avg)
Session intensity peaks at era 8 (The Crucible) and era 6 (Quiet), suggesting deep work periods
The last three eras (7-9) show increasing session size as the project matures from implementation to evaluation to documentation

message_statistics.description: Aggregate statistics on human messages across all sessions
message_statistics.note: human-messages.json contains 58 sessions (subset of 71 total from era chunks; era1/era2 are empty, and some era3-9 sessions appear in overlapping chunk files)

very_short_examples: wire up the compost CLI subcommand
long_context_carriers: Implementation plans with full code snippets (3000+ chars)

derived_insights: Mean session has ~20 human messages, indicating sustained multi-turn interactions rather than single-shot commands
4 sessions exceeded 50 messages - these are marathon problem-solving sessions (era6 autonomous planning, era8 dogfooding)
The 5 single-message sessions are likely continuation prompts or quick check-ins that relied on context from prior sessions
Message length is bimodal: many terse directives ('fix it', 'wire it up') mixed with detailed architectural descriptions
The developer communicates in a high-directness, low-formality style - rarely uses formal language, often uses imperative mood

frustration_analysis.description: Keyword-based frustration detection across all session messages. Counts represent line-level matches in human-messages.json.

wiring_problem.description: Agent builds modules but fails to connect them end-to-end
key_quotes: Anything that is not wired up needs to be wired up. I really don't understand what this instruction I am not telling you is, because it happens every time with every coding agent.

hallucination_problem.description: Agent loses context between sessions or context-drifts to wrong task
key_quotes: this is not at all what we were fucking talking about. What plan is this?
I don't remember the fucking document, but I know that this was supposed to have persistent memory.

ralph_loop_not_working.description: Core iterative loop stops prematurely instead of iterating until quality achieved
key_quotes: There is a Ralph loop. The Ralph loop, by definition, keeps working until the thing is good. If something ends at point 18, that means that something is not working.
We spent a lot of time working on this, and it's still not working as intended.

premature_victory_declaration.description: Agent claims something works when it visibly does not
key_quotes: Again, the same fucking problem. I cannot see any of the fucking examples because I just see a white square.
Need to fix your fucking lying. You added a bunch of stats, and all the fucking examples you put in were the old screenshots.

model_discovery_problem.description: Agent cannot find or use locally loaded LLM models
key_quotes: Don't be a little bitch. I literally have two models loaded, so fucking find them.
Why are you being so difficult with me? The models are already loaded.

emotional_arc.days_21_to_22_mar_21_22: Quality crusade - Channeling frustration into audits (era4)
emotional_arc.day_30_mar_30: THE CRUCIBLE - Peak frustration across all categories (era8)
emotional_arc.day_32_apr_1: THE BIBLE - Calm. Documentation. Narrative mining. (era9)

derived_insights: 11 instances of fuck/fucking across 920 messages (1.2% of messages) - not casual profanity, always tied to specific system failures
Frustration is always productive: every significant frustration event led to permanent automated enforcement infrastructure
The frustration curve is U-shaped: peaks at era3 (wiring) and era8 (dogfooding), with a calm middle period
The developer does not express frustration at the project itself, only at agent behavior patterns (hallucination, not wiring, premature victory)
Frustration vocabulary is consistent and targeted: 'fucking' modifies the specific broken thing, not used as generic expression
The wiring problem is the foundational frustration - it spawned 3 hooks, a memory entry, and the entire 'always wire everything up' philosophy

intent_analysis.description: What the user asked for most, measured by keyword frequency in human-messages.json. Counts are line-level matches (a single message may match multiple intents).

execution_and_verification.description: Running, testing, building, and fixing. The majority of interaction is about executing and verifying.

creation_and_generation.description: Creating new things - code, features, modules, outputs.

integration_and_wiring.description: Connecting components, committing code, integrating systems.

planning_and_review.description: Strategic work: planning architecture, auditing quality, reviewing designs.

top_user_intents_narrative.evidence: 366 keyword hits. The developer is deeply hands-on, constantly running and testing.
signature_quotes: ok so trest and geberate all the tings
I need you to test even more. I need you to test the entire possibility space

top_user_intents_narrative.evidence: 153 keyword hits. Creative output is the core purpose of Liminal.
signature_quotes: test even more... test the entire possibility space

top_user_intents_narrative.evidence: 125 keyword hits. The wiring imperative is the developer's signature concern.
signature_quotes: wire up the compost CLI subcommand
Anything that is not wired up needs to be wired up

top_user_intents_narrative.evidence: 58 keyword hits. Periodic deep audits and architectural reviews.
signature_quotes: I want you to do a complete adversarial audit
I need you to audit the entire history of liminal

derived_insights: The developer is execution-oriented: 52% of intent signals are about running/testing/building/fixing, not planning
'Wire' appears 22 times as a keyword - this is disproportionately high for a single technical concept and reflects the core philosophy
'Commit' (44) appears more than 'plan' (14), suggesting a bias toward action over deliberation
The intent profile matches a 'builder' personality: create, execute, fix, commit. Minimal time spent on design/refactor/debug
'Refactor' appears only 2 times, 'debug' only 1 time - the developer prefers to rebuild rather than incrementally fix
'Deploy' appears only 1 time - the project is local-first, focused on creative output rather than deployment

era_intensity.description: Data volume per era as proxy for development intensity

era5-conversational.note: Largest data volume of any era

era6-quiet.note: Smallest data volume but very high per-session message count

era7-multimedia.note: High bytes-per-session suggests large code blocks / implementation plans

intensity_ranking.metric: 205 KB data volume - largest of any era
174 KB with 27 sessions - most sessions of any era
123 KB in a single day (Mar 20) - highest daily intensity
121 KB with only 5 sessions - very dense per-session output
42 KB with peak frustration - The Crucible

derived_insights: Total data volume is 722 KB across 58 sessions spanning 13 active days
Era 5 (Conversational) has the largest data volume (200 KB) - this was the period of most active human-agent dialogue
Era 3 (Consolidation) had the highest daily intensity - 123 KB produced in a single day (Mar 20)
Era 7 (Multimedia) has the highest bytes-per-session (24.7 KB) - large implementation plans and code blocks
The 4-day gap (Mar 25-28) between era6 and era7 is the only significant gap in the development timeline
Data volume does not correlate with session count: era7 had only 5 sessions but 148 messages (dense implementation work)

time_analysis.description: Temporal patterns across the development timeline

peak_day.note: The quality era peak. Saturday with sustained coding across the entire day.

work_pattern.description: Session timestamps clustered by time of day
work_pattern.insight: Primarily an evening/night developer. 60% of sessions started after 6pm. Peak activity between 6pm and midnight.

derived_insights: The project was built in 13 calendar days with 9 active coding days
March 22 was the most productive day with 15 sessions (era4/era5 overlap)
The 4-day gap (Mar 25-28) coincides with era6 ending and era7 beginning - likely a processing period
Late-night sessions (midnight to 6am) account for 12% of sessions - the developer works past midnight regularly
No weekend/weekday pattern is visible: Mar 20 was a Friday, Mar 22 was a Sunday, Mar 29 was a Sunday
The development pace accelerated over time: first week had many short sessions, later eras had fewer but deeper sessions

the_liminal_developer_profile.communication_style: High-directness, low-formality. Imperative mood. Mix of terse directives and detailed architectural descriptions. Uses profanity exclusively tied to specific system failures, never casual.
the_liminal_developer_profile.working_pattern: Evening/night focused. 60% of sessions after 6pm. Marathon sessions (4 sessions over 50 messages). Prefers deep sustained work over many short sessions.
core_values: End-to-end wiring: Everything must be connected and working
Brutal honesty: Never claim something works when it doesn't
Iterative quality: The loop keeps going until quality is achieved
Emotion-to-automation: Every frustration becomes permanent infrastructure
the_liminal_developer_profile.frustration_pattern: Frustration is always productive and targeted. Each frustration event led to automated enforcement (9 hooks). The developer converts emotional responses into systematic solutions.
the_liminal_developer_profile.development_philosophy: Build first, audit second, wire always. The cycle is: build features, encounter integration gaps, get frustrated that things aren't wired, create enforcement hooks, move forward with stronger guardrails.

recommended_telemetry_improvements: Quality-of-output tracking: Win/loss tracks bash exit codes but not whether generated art was actually good
Frustration-to-fix latency: Track time between frustration expression and corresponding fix commit
Context continuity score: Track how often agent references previous sessions vs. hallucinates
Ralph Loop completion rate: Track iterations run vs. early terminations
Wiring coverage: Track percentage of built modules actually wired into the system
